install: Download from https://www.python.org/downloads/ (Windows/macOS installers; on Windows check "Add Python to PATH"). On Debian/Ubuntu: sudo apt install python3 python3-venv python3-pip. Verify: python3 --version (or `python --version` on Windows). Create an isolated project env: python3 -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate (Unix/macOS) or python -m venv .venv; .venv\Scripts\Activate.ps1 (Windows PowerShell). Install packages: python -m pip install <package>.
quickstart: 1) Create/activate a venv: `python3 -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate` (Windows PowerShell: `python -m venv .venv; .venv\Scripts\Activate.ps1`).; 2) Write hello.py:; ```python; def greet(name: str) -> str:;     return f"Hello, {name}!"; ; if __name__ == "__main__":;     print(greet("world")); ```; 3) Run it: `python hello.py`  ->  Hello, world!; 4) Start an interactive REPL with `python` (3.13+ ships a multi-line, color-highlighted REPL).
idioms: Type hints + the 3.12 PEP 695 generics syntax: `def first[T](xs: list[T]) -> T: return xs[0]` and `type Vector = list[float]` for type aliases (replaces explicit TypeVar/TypeAlias boilerplate).
f-strings for formatting, now with PEP 701 freedom to reuse quotes and nest: `f"items: {", ".join(names)}"` and `f"{value:.2f}"`; use `f"{x=}"` for quick debug output.
Context managers with `with` for deterministic cleanup: `with open(path, encoding="utf-8") as f: data = f.read()` — guarantees the file closes even on exceptions.

Comprehensions and generator expressions for data transforms: `[x*x for x in nums if x > 0]`, `{k: v for k, v in pairs}`, and lazy `(line.strip() for line in f)`; 3.12 inlines comprehensions for up to ~2x speedup.
Structural pattern matching with `match`/`case` for dispatch on shape: `match cmd:;     case {"op": "move", "x": x, "y": y}: ...;     case _: ...`.
Standard-library-first toolset: `pathlib.Path` for filesystem paths, `dataclasses.dataclass` for plain data containers, `enum.Enum` for constants, and `argparse` for CLIs.

Concurrency: `asyncio` with `async def`/`await` (`asyncio.run(main())`) for I/O-bound work, and `concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor`/`ProcessPoolExecutor` for parallelism; 3.13 adds an experimental free-threaded (no-GIL) build.
when_to_use: A high-level, general-purpose, dynamically typed language ideal for scripting, automation, data/ML, web backends, and CLIs where readability and a vast standard library plus PyPI ecosystem outweigh raw runtime speed.

install: Standalone installer (recommended) — macOS/Linux: curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh ; Windows (PowerShell): powershell -ExecutionPolicy ByPass -c "irm https://astral.sh/uv/install.ps1 | iex". Alternatives: pipx install uv, brew install uv, or pip install uv.
quickstart: 1) Create a project: `uv init myapp && cd myapp`; 2) Add dependencies (updates pyproject.toml + uv.lock + the .venv environment): `uv add requests 'rich>=13'`; 3) Run code in the project environment (auto-syncs first): `uv run main.py` (or `uv run python -c "import requests"`); 4) Reproduce the locked environment on another machine: `uv sync`; You never manually create/activate a venv or call pip — uv manages .venv and the cross-platform uv.lock for you.
idioms: Project workflow: `uv init`, `uv add <pkg>` / `uv remove <pkg>` (auto-updates pyproject.toml, uv.lock, and .venv), then `uv run <cmd>` which verifies the lockfile and environment are current before executing.
Dev/optional dependencies and upgrades: `uv add --dev pytest` for tooling deps; `uv lock --upgrade-package requests` to bump a single locked package; `uv sync` to make .venv exactly match uv.lock.

Run tools ephemerally without installing via `uvx <tool>` (alias for `uv tool run`), e.g. `uvx ruff`; persistently install CLIs onto PATH with `uv tool install ruff` and keep them current with `uv tool upgrade --all`.

Standalone scripts with PEP 723 inline metadata: `uv add --script example.py 'requests<3' 'rich'` writes a `# /// script` dependency block, then `uv run example.py` builds the env automatically; use `uv run --with rich example.py` for one-off deps without editing the file.

Manage Python interpreters directly: `uv python install 3.12` (and `3.11 3.12` or `pypy@3.10`), `uv python list` to see versions, `uv python pin 3.12` to pin a project version; uv auto-downloads required versions on demand.

pip-compatible drop-in layer for lower-level control: `uv venv` to create an environment, `uv pip install <pkg>`, `uv pip compile requirements.in -o requirements.txt` (pip-tools style), and `uv pip sync requirements.txt` — note uv reimplements this interface and does not invoke pip.
Build and publish packages: `uv build` produces the sdist/wheel and `uv publish` uploads to an index (PyPI by default).
when_to_use: Use uv as a single, extremely fast Rust-based tool to replace pip, pip-tools, virtualenv, pipx, and Poetry/PDM/Rye for managing Python versions, virtual environments, project dependencies (with a reproducible lockfile), tools, and scripts.

install: pip install "fastapi[standard]"   # includes uvicorn + the `fastapi` CLI. Use `pip install fastapi` for the slim core, or `pip install "fastapi[standard-no-fastapi-cloud-cli]"` to omit the cloud CLI.
quickstart: 1. (Optional) Create & activate a virtual environment.; 2. Install: pip install "fastapi[standard]"; 3. Create a file named main.py:; ; from fastapi import FastAPI; ; app = FastAPI(); ; @app.get("/"); async def read_root():;     return {"Hello": "World"}; ; @app.get("/items/{item_id}"); async def read_item(item_id: int, q: str | None = None):;     return {"item_id": item_id, "q": q}; ; 4. Run the dev server: fastapi dev;    (serves at http://127.0.0.1:8000, auto-reload on); 5. Open interactive docs at http://127.0.0.1:8000/docs (Swagger UI) or /redoc (ReDoc).
idioms: Path & query params via type hints: function args become typed params with automatic validation/coercion. Path: `item_id: int` from `/items/{item_id}`; query: `q: str | None = None` (a default makes it an optional query param).

Request bodies with Pydantic: subclass `pydantic.BaseModel` (e.g. `class Item(BaseModel): name: str; price: float`) and declare it as a parameter (`async def create_item(item: Item):`); FastAPI parses JSON, validates, and documents it automatically.

Dependency injection with `Depends` + `Annotated`: define a dependency function and inject it, ideally via a reusable alias, e.g. `CommonsDep = Annotated[dict, Depends(common_parameters)]` then `async def read_items(commons: CommonsDep):`. Used for shared params, DB sessions, auth.
Response shaping: use `@app.get(..., response_model=Item)` or a typed return annotation to filter/validate/document output, and `status_code=201` to set the HTTP status of a path operation.

Async vs sync: declare `async def` for endpoints/dependencies doing awaitable I/O (await async clients); use plain `def` for blocking/sync code and FastAPI runs it in a threadpool so the event loop is not blocked.
Organize with APIRouter: split endpoints across modules using `router = APIRouter()` and mount them via `app.include_router(router, prefix="/items", tags=["items"])`.
Error handling: raise `from fastapi import HTTPException` with `raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="Not found")` to return proper error responses with structured JSON.
when_to_use: Use FastAPI to build high-performance, type-safe Python REST/JSON APIs (and async backends) with automatic data validation and interactive OpenAPI docs.

install: pip install pydantic   # extras: pip install 'pydantic[email,timezone]'  | uv: uv add pydantic  | conda: conda install pydantic -c conda-forge  (requires Python 3.9+; current v2.13.x)
quickstart: from datetime import datetime; from pydantic import BaseModel, PositiveInt, ValidationError; ; class User(BaseModel):;     id: int;     name: str = 'John Doe';     signup_ts: datetime | None = None;     tastes: dict[str, PositiveInt]; ; # Coercion + validation from external (e.g. JSON-decoded) data; user = User(id='123', signup_ts='2019-06-01 12:22', tastes={'wine': 9, 'cabbage': '1'}); print(user.id)            # 123 (coerced str -> int); print(user.model_dump())  # plain dict; ; try:;     User(id='not-an-int', tastes={}); except ValidationError as e:;     print(e)  # one exception aggregating all field errors; e.errors() for structured details
idioms: Define schemas as type-hinted classes subclassing BaseModel; fields with a default (`name: str = 'x'`) are optional, those without are required. Pydantic coerces compatible inputs (e.g. '123' -> 123) by default.

Parse untrusted/raw input with the classmethods `Model.model_validate(obj)` (dict/obj), `Model.model_validate_json(json_str)` (parse + validate JSON directly), and `Model.model_validate_strings(...)`; the plain constructor `Model(**data)` works for kwargs.

Serialize with `instance.model_dump()` -> dict and `instance.model_dump_json()` -> JSON string; control output with `exclude_none=True`, `exclude_unset=True`, `by_alias=True`, `mode='json'`, or `include`/`exclude` sets.
Customize fields via `Field(...)`, typically with `Annotated[int, Field(gt=0, alias='userId', description=...)]`; use `default_factory` for mutable defaults (e.g. `Field(default_factory=list)`).

Add custom logic with `@field_validator('name', mode='before'|'after')` (decorate a `@classmethod`) for single fields, and `@model_validator(mode='after')` (operates on `self`) for cross-field checks like password confirmation; raise `ValueError`/`AssertionError` to signal failures.

Configure model behavior with `model_config = ConfigDict(...)`: common keys are `extra='forbid'|'allow'|'ignore'`, `frozen=True` (immutable/hashable), `str_strip_whitespace=True`, `populate_by_name=True`, and `from_attributes=True` (validate ORM-style objects).

Validate standalone (non-model) types with `TypeAdapter(list[int])` and its `.validate_python()` / `.validate_json()` / `.dump_python()`; always catch `pydantic.ValidationError` and inspect `e.errors()` for machine-readable error lists. Use `model_construct()` only to bypass validation for already-trusted data.
when_to_use: Use Pydantic v2 to define type-hinted data models that validate, coerce, and serialize untrusted input (API payloads, config, LLM/tool outputs) at runtime, backed by a fast Rust core.

install: pip install "sqlalchemy[asyncio]"   # plus an async driver, e.g. asyncpg for Postgres or aiosqlite for SQLite: pip install asyncpg aiosqlite
quickstart: import asyncio; from sqlalchemy import select; from sqlalchemy.orm import DeclarativeBase, Mapped, mapped_column; from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import (;     create_async_engine, async_sessionmaker, AsyncSession,; ); ; class Base(DeclarativeBase):;     pass; ; class User(Base):;     __tablename__ = "user";     id: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(primary_key=True);     name: Mapped[str]; ; async def main():;     # Note the async driver in the URL: postgresql+asyncpg / sqlite+aiosqlite;     engine = create_async_engine("sqlite+aiosqlite://", echo=True); ;     # DDL runs sync code over the async connection via run_sync;     async with engine.begin() as conn:;         await conn.run_sync(Base.metadata.create_all); ;     # expire_on_commit=False so attributes stay usable after commit;     Session = async_sessionmaker(engine, expire_on_commit=False); ;     async with Session() as session:;         async with session.begin():            # begin/commit transaction;             session.add(User(name="alice")); ;         result = await session.execute(select(User))  # 2.0-style query;         users = result.scalars().all();         print(users); ;     await engine.dispose(); ; asyncio.run(main())
idioms: Always use an async DBAPI driver in the URL: 'postgresql+asyncpg://...', 'mysql+asyncmy://...', or 'sqlite+aiosqlite://...'; create_async_engine() requires it.

await every IO call: 'await session.execute(select(...))', 'await session.scalars(...)', 'await session.commit()', 'await session.get(Model, pk)'; use 'async with' for engine/session/transaction context managers.
Use 2.0-style querying — 'session.execute(select(Model))' then '.scalars().all()/.first()' — instead of the legacy 'session.query()' API.
Avoid implicit/lazy IO: eager-load relationships up front with options like 'selectinload(A.bs)' (or joinedload/subqueryload), since lazy loading raises in async context.
Set 'expire_on_commit=False' on async_sessionmaker so committed objects' attributes remain accessible without triggering an implicit (illegal) lazy load.
For occasional on-demand lazy access, add the 'AsyncAttrs' mixin to your Base and use 'await obj.awaitable_attrs.relationship_name'.
Run sync-only operations (DDL, metadata.create_all, legacy code) over the async connection with 'await conn.run_sync(...)'; use 'async with engine.begin()' for a transactional connection.
Concurrency: a single AsyncSession is NOT safe across concurrent tasks — create one AsyncSession per task (e.g. per request/asyncio.gather branch) from a shared async_sessionmaker/engine.
when_to_use: Use when building non-blocking, IO-concurrent apps (e.g. asyncio/FastAPI services) that need ORM/Core access to async-capable databases via an async driver.

install: Linux (official PGDG apt repo, Debian/Ubuntu): `sudo apt install -y postgresql-common && sudo /usr/share/postgresql-common/pgdg/apt.postgresql.org.sh && sudo apt update && sudo apt install postgresql-18`. macOS: `brew install postgresql@18` or use Postgres.app. Windows/macOS GUI: download the EDB-certified installer from https://www.postgresql.org/download/. Current stable major version is PostgreSQL 18 (18.4 as of mid-2026).
quickstart: 1. Create a database (shell tools shipped with the server):;    $ createdb mydb; ; 2. Open an interactive session against it:;    $ psql mydb;    psql (18.4);    Type "help" for help.;    mydb=>; ; 3. Run SQL at the prompt (statements end with a semicolon):;    mydb=> CREATE TABLE weather (city varchar(80), temp_lo int, temp_hi int, prcp real, date date);;    mydb=> INSERT INTO weather VALUES ('San Francisco', 46, 50, 0.25, '1994-11-27');;    mydb=> SELECT * FROM weather WHERE city = 'San Francisco';; ; 4. Useful psql meta-commands: \h <CMD> (SQL help), \? (list psql commands), \dt (list tables), \q (quit).; ; 5. Remove a database when done:;    $ dropdb mydb
idioms: Connect/inspect with psql meta-commands: \l (list databases), \dt / \d <table> (describe), \du (roles), \c <db> (switch DB), \timing, and \copy for client-side CSV import/export.
Use upsert via INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (col) DO UPDATE SET ... / DO NOTHING to merge rows idempotently on a unique constraint.
Reach for native JSONB columns with operators (->, ->>, @>, jsonb_path_query) and index them with GIN for flexible/semi-structured data instead of EAV tables.

Index intentionally: B-tree for equality/range, GIN for JSONB/array/full-text (tsvector), partial indexes (WHERE ...) and expression indexes for targeted query speedups; run EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) to validate plans.
Wrap multi-statement changes in BEGIN; ... COMMIT; (or ROLLBACK;), and use RETURNING to get generated keys/updated rows back in one round trip.
Use window functions (ROW_NUMBER(), RANK(), SUM(...) OVER (PARTITION BY ...)) and CTEs (WITH ...) for analytics and readable multi-step queries; add LATERAL joins for per-row subqueries.

Manage schema evolution with transactional DDL (most ALTER TABLE / CREATE run inside a transaction), and use generated columns, CHECK constraints, and proper FOREIGN KEY ... ON DELETE actions to keep data integrity in the database.
Operate with roles/GRANT for least-privilege access, schemas for namespacing, and EXTENSIONs (CREATE EXTENSION) such as pg_stat_statements, pgcrypto, or PostGIS to extend capabilities.
when_to_use: Use PostgreSQL as a general-purpose, ACID-compliant relational database when you need strong data integrity, rich SQL, and extensibility (JSONB, full-text, geospatial, custom types) for transactional or mixed analytical workloads.

install: pip install -U pytest   # verify with: pytest --version  (latest 9.1.1, Jun 2026)
quickstart: 1. Install: `pip install -U pytest`; 2. Create a file named test_sample.py (test files match test_*.py or *_test.py):; ; ```python; # content of test_sample.py; def inc(x):;     return x + 1; ; def test_answer():;     assert inc(3) == 5; ```; ; 3. Run the whole suite from the project root:; ; ```; $ pytest; ```; ; pytest auto-discovers test_* functions and Test* classes, runs them, and on failure prints detailed assertion introspection (here it shows inc(3) == 4, not 5). Run a single file/test with `pytest test_sample.py` or `pytest test_sample.py::test_answer`.
idioms: Plain assert with introspection: write `assert expr` (e.g. `assert a == b`) - pytest rewrites asserts to show actual values on failure; no assertEqual-style methods needed.

Fixtures for setup/teardown: define reusable setup with `@pytest.fixture` and request it by adding the fixture name as a test argument. Use `yield` inside the fixture to return the value and run teardown code after the yield.

Fixture scope and sharing: control how often a fixture runs with `@pytest.fixture(scope="function"|"class"|"module"|"package"|"session")`; put shared fixtures in conftest.py so they are auto-discovered without imports.

Parametrize tests: stack `@pytest.mark.parametrize("arg1,arg2", [(in, expected), ...])` over a test to run it once per case; stacking multiple parametrize decorators produces the cartesian product of cases.
Assert exceptions: use `with pytest.raises(ValueError): ...` (optionally `match="regex"` or `as excinfo`) to assert a block raises the expected exception.

Skip and xfail: mark tests with `@pytest.mark.skip(reason=...)`, conditionally with `@pytest.mark.skipif(cond, reason=...)`, or mark known failures with `@pytest.mark.xfail(reason=..., raises=..., strict=True)`.

Selective runs and reporting: filter tests with `pytest -k "expr"` (name substring) or `pytest -m markername` (custom markers), and use `-x` (stop on first failure), `-q`/`-v` (quiet/verbose), and `-s` (show prints) day-to-day.
when_to_use: Use pytest as the de-facto framework for writing and running automated tests in Python - from tiny unit tests to large functional suites - thanks to plain-assert syntax, powerful fixtures, and a rich plugin ecosystem.

install: pip install ruff   (or: uv add --dev ruff). Standalone installers — macOS/Linux: curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/ruff/install.sh | sh ; Windows: powershell -c "irm https://astral.sh/ruff/install.ps1 | iex". Also available via Homebrew, Conda, and other package managers.
quickstart: Install: pip install ruff; Lint the current directory (or a path): ruff check .; Auto-fix the fixable violations: ruff check . --fix; Format the code (Black-compatible formatter): ruff format .; Target a specific file: ruff check src/app.py; Add a [tool.ruff] section to pyproject.toml to configure, then re-run. Both commands exit non-zero on remaining issues, making them CI-friendly.
idioms: Lint then format as two distinct commands: `ruff check` runs the linter (Flake8/isort/pyupgrade replacement), `ruff format` runs the Black-compatible formatter — they are separate tools sharing one binary.
Auto-fix safely with `ruff check --fix`; use `--fix --unsafe-fixes` for corrections that may change behavior, and `--show-fixes` to list what was changed.

Configure in `[tool.ruff]` (global: `line-length`, `target-version`, `exclude`) and `[tool.ruff.lint]` (`select`, `ignore`, `fixable`/`unfixable`) within pyproject.toml; or use a standalone `ruff.toml`/`.ruff.toml`. A common starter select is ["E", "F", "I"] (pycodestyle errors, Pyflakes, isort).
Scope rules per path with `[tool.ruff.lint.per-file-ignores]`, e.g. "__init__.py" = ["F401"] and "**/{tests}/*" = ["E402"], so test/init files relax specific rules.
Suppress a single line inline with a `# noqa: F401` comment (or bare `# noqa`); let Ruff insert these automatically across a file with `ruff check --add-noqa`.

Wire into CI/pre-commit using the astral-sh/ruff-pre-commit repo with `id: ruff-check` (add `args: [--fix]`) and `id: ruff-format`; bare `ruff check`/`ruff format --check` return non-zero on failures for gating.
Select/override rules ad hoc from the CLI without editing config: `ruff check --select F401,I --ignore E501`, or `--config "lint.dummy-variable-rgx = '__.*'"` for one-off TOML overrides.
when_to_use: Use Ruff as a single, extremely fast (Rust-based) drop-in replacement for Flake8, isort, pyupgrade, and Black to lint and format Python code locally and in CI.

install: Create a new app with a recommended framework: `npx create-next-app@latest` (Next.js) or `npx create-react-router@latest` (React Router v7) or `npx create-expo-app@latest` (native). To add to an existing project / install directly: `npm install react react-dom`. (Create React App is deprecated as of Feb 2025.)
quickstart:
// A function component using React 19 idioms; import { useActionState } from "react"; ; function UpdateName() {; const [error, submitAction, isPending] = useActionState(; async (previousState, formData) => {; const error = await updateName(formData.get("name"));; return error || null;; },; null; ); ; return (;
; ; ; {error &&

{error}

};
; );; }; ; // Mount with the root API:; import { createRoot } from "react-dom/client"; createRoot(document.getElementById("root")).render();
idioms: Forms via Actions: pass an async function to a <form action={fn}> and manage its lifecycle with useActionState, which returns [state, formAction, isPending] - no more manual isLoading/error flags.
Show instant UI feedback during async submissions with useOptimistic(currentValue), updating the optimistic value inside the action before the request resolves.
Read parent <form> submission status from nested components with const {pending} = useFormStatus() instead of prop drilling.
Read promises and context during render with the new use(resource) API: use(promise) suspends until it resolves; use(Context) can be called conditionally (after early returns), unlike useContext.
Pass ref directly as a prop to function components - forwardRef is no longer needed; ref callbacks can also return a cleanup function.
Render <title>, <meta>, and <link> tags directly inside components; React auto-hoists them to <head>. Use <Context value={...}> directly as a provider instead of <Context.Provider>.

Call Server Functions from Client Components with the 'use server' directive, and optimize loading with react-dom preloading APIs (preload, preinit, preconnect, prefetchDNS). React 19.2 adds <Activity>, useEffectEvent, and partial pre-rendering.
when_to_use: Use React 19 to build interactive UIs and full-stack web/native apps when you want first-class async Actions, form state hooks, Server Components, and built-in document-metadata/resource handling.

install: npm install typescript --save-dev   # per-project (recommended); run via `npx tsc`; # or globally: npm install -g typescript; npx tsc --init   # generate a tsconfig.json
quickstart: 1. Add TypeScript to a project: `npm install typescript --save-dev`; 2. Generate config: `npx tsc --init` (creates tsconfig.json).; 3. Write a typed file, e.g. hello.ts:; ;    interface User {;      name: string;;      id: number;;    }; ;    function greet(user: User): string {;      return `Hello, ${user.name} (#${user.id})`;;    }; ;    const me: User = { name: "Ada", id: 1 };;    console.log(greet(me)); ; 4. Compile to JavaScript: `npx tsc` (compiles per tsconfig.json) or `npx tsc hello.ts` for a single file.; 5. Run the emitted JS with Node: `node hello.js`. Type errors are reported at compile time, before you run.
idioms: Annotate values, params, and return types with `: Type` (e.g. `function f(x: number): string`); lean on type inference for locals like `let s = "hi"` (inferred string) and only annotate where it adds clarity or at API boundaries.

Model object shapes with `interface` (extendable, good for object/class contracts) and use `type` aliases for unions, intersections, tuples, and literal types: `type State = "open" | "closed" | "minimized"`.

Use union types plus narrowing to handle multiple shapes safely: `function len(x: string | string[]) { return typeof x === "string" ? x.length : x.length }` — guards like `typeof`, `in`, and `instanceof` refine the type per branch.
Write reusable, type-safe code with generics: `function first<T>(arr: T[]): T | undefined { return arr[0] }` and generic interfaces like `interface Box<T> { value: T }`.
Rely on structural typing: compatibility is by shape, not by name — an object literal `{ x: 12, y: 26 }` satisfies `interface Point { x: number; y: number }` without explicitly implementing it.
Configure the compiler via tsconfig.json; enable `"strict": true` for full type-safety (strictNullChecks, noImplicitAny, etc.) and set `target`, `module`, `outDir`, and `rootDir` to control emit.
Use utility types to transform existing types instead of redefining them: `Partial<T>`, `Pick<T, K>`, `Omit<T, K>`, `Record<K, V>`, `Readonly<T>`, and `ReturnType<typeof fn>`.
when_to_use: Use TypeScript when you want static type checking, richer editor tooling, and safer refactoring on top of JavaScript for any project that grows beyond trivial scripts.

install: Vite (recommended): npm install tailwindcss @tailwindcss/vite — then add tailwindcss() to vite.config.ts plugins and put @import "tailwindcss"; in your CSS. PostCSS (Next.js/Angular): npm install tailwindcss @tailwindcss/postcss postcss with @tailwindcss/postcss in postcss.config.mjs.
quickstart:
1) Scaffold: npm create vite@latest my-project && cd my-project; 2) Install: npm install tailwindcss @tailwindcss/vite; 3) Configure vite.config.ts:; import { defineConfig } from 'vite'; import tailwindcss from '@tailwindcss/vite'; export default defineConfig({ plugins: [tailwindcss()] }); 4) Add to your main CSS file: @import "tailwindcss";; 5) Run: npm run dev; 6) Use utility classes in markup:;

Hello world!

; (In v4 there is no tailwind.config.js by default and source files are auto-detected — no content config required.)
idioms: Compose utilities directly in markup (utility-first): class="text-3xl font-bold underline" — no separate CSS file per component
Mobile-first responsive design via breakpoint prefixes that apply at that size and up: class="text-sm md:text-base lg:text-lg"
State and interaction variants as prefixes: hover:underline, focus:ring, disabled:opacity-50, group-hover:bg-gray-100, and stacked variants like md:hover:bg-blue-600

Dark mode with the dark: variant (defaults to prefers-color-scheme): class="bg-white dark:bg-gray-800 text-gray-900 dark:text-white". For class-based toggling add @custom-variant dark (&:where(.dark, .dark *)); to your CSS and put class="dark" on <html>

Customize the design system in CSS with @theme using CSS variables — @theme { --color-brand: oklch(0.72 0.11 178); --font-display: 'Inter', sans-serif; --breakpoint-3xl: 120rem; } — which generates utilities like bg-brand, font-display, 3xl:*
Escape hatch for one-off values via arbitrary value/property brackets: top-[117px], bg-[#bada55], text-[22px], [mask-type:luminance], and CSS-variable shorthand fill-(--my-brand-color)

Extract reusable component classes when needed with @layer components plus theme variables: @layer components { .card { background-color: var(--color-white); border-radius: var(--radius-lg); padding: --spacing(6); } }
when_to_use: Use Tailwind CSS when you want to build and maintain custom UI rapidly by composing utility classes directly in markup, with a small zero-runtime CSS output and a consistent design-token system, instead of hand-writing bespoke CSS or relying on a prescriptive component library.

install: npm create vite@latest; # or scaffold non-interactively with a template:; npm create vite@latest my-app -- --template react-ts; # then:; cd my-app && npm install && npm run dev; # Requires Node.js 20.19+ or 22.12+. Also works with yarn/pnpm/bun create vite.
quickstart: 1. Scaffold: `npm create vite@latest my-app` (pick a framework template such as vanilla, vue, react, svelte, solid, lit, qwik, and their `-ts` variants).; 2. Install and run: `cd my-app && npm install && npm run dev` -> dev server at http://localhost:5173.; 3. `index.html` at the project root is the entry point and part of the module graph; it references your app via `<script type="module" src="/src/main.js"></script>`.; 4. Default package.json scripts: `"dev": "vite"` (dev server with HMR), `"build": "vite build"` (production bundle), `"preview": "vite preview"` (serve the built output locally).; 5. Optional config in `vite.config.js`/`vite.config.ts` using `defineConfig({ plugins: [...] })`.
idioms: Bare/NPM imports just work: `import { x } from 'my-dep'` is resolved and pre-bundled (CJS/UMD converted to ESM) automatically; no manual bundler config for dependencies.

Import assets to get a resolved URL: `import logo from './logo.png'`. Use query suffixes `?url` (explicit URL), `?raw` (file contents as a string), and `?worker` (Web Worker) to change import behavior.

CSS and CSS Modules: `import './style.css'` injects styles with HMR; `import styles from './x.module.css'` returns an object of hashed class names. Sass/Less/Stylus work by installing the preprocessor package.

Env vars via `import.meta.env`: only keys prefixed with `VITE_` are exposed to client code (e.g. `import.meta.env.VITE_API_URL`). Built-ins: `MODE`, `BASE_URL`, `PROD`, `DEV`, `SSR`. Loaded from `.env`, `.env.local`, `.env.[mode]`, `.env.[mode].local`. Never put secrets in `VITE_*` -- they are bundled into the build.
Glob imports: `import.meta.glob('./modules/*.js')` lazy-loads matched files; pass `{ eager: true }` for direct (eagerly bundled) imports. Supports named imports and negative patterns.
TypeScript and JSON work out of the box: `.ts` files are transpiled (type-checking is left to your IDE/`tsc --noEmit`); JSON supports default and named (tree-shakeable) imports.
Production builds use Rollup/Rolldown under the hood and emit optimized, code-split static assets; verify locally with `vite preview` before deploying.
when_to_use: Use Vite as the dev server and build tool for modern web/SPA projects (vanilla or framework-based) when you want near-instant startup, fast HMR via native ESM in dev, and an optimized bundled production build.

install: Recommended (via nvm, from nodejs.org/en/download):;   curl -o- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nvm-sh/nvm/v0.40.3/install.sh | bash;   nvm install 24        # current LTS line (v24.x); use `nvm install --lts`;   node -v && npm -v     # verify; ; Or download an OS installer / prebuilt binary from https://nodejs.org/en/download (LTS recommended).
quickstart: 1. Save a file, e.g. server.js:; ;    const { createServer } = require('node:http');;    const server = createServer((req, res) => {;      res.statusCode = 200;;      res.setHeader('Content-Type', 'text/plain');;      res.end('Hello World');;    });;    server.listen(3000, '127.0.0.1', () => {;      console.log('Server running at http://127.0.0.1:3000/');;    }); ; 2. Run it:  node server.js; 3. Visit http://127.0.0.1:3000/; ; Run any script with `node <file>.js`. Init a package with `npm init -y`, install deps with `npm install <pkg>`.
idioms: Import core modules with the `node:` prefix (e.g. `require('node:http')`, `import fs from 'node:fs'`) to be explicit about built-ins vs npm packages.
Choose a module system: CommonJS (`require`/`module.exports`) or ESM (`import`/`export`). Use `.mjs` or set `"type": "module"` in package.json for ESM, `.cjs` for CommonJS.
Prefer promise-based core APIs: `import { readFile } from 'node:fs/promises'` and `await readFile(path, 'utf8')` instead of callback APIs.
Manage projects with npm: `npm init -y`, `npm install <pkg>` (deps) / `npm install -D <pkg>` (dev), and define `scripts` in package.json run via `npm run <name>`.
Read configuration from `process.env` (environment variables); Node 20+ can load a .env file directly with `node --env-file=.env app.js`.
Use the built-in test runner: write tests with `import { test } from 'node:test'` plus `node:assert`, then run `node --test`.
Handle async errors with try/catch around `await`, and listen for `process.on('uncaughtException')` / `unhandledRejection` to fail safely.
Use a version manager (nvm/fnm) plus an `.nvmrc` file to pin and switch Node versions per project.
when_to_use: Use Node.js to run JavaScript/TypeScript outside the browser for servers, APIs, CLIs, build tooling, and I/O-bound backend services.

install: npm install express   # latest is Express 5.x (5.1.0 is the default on npm); requires Node.js 18+
quickstart: // app.js (CommonJS); const express = require('express'); const app = express(); const port = 3000; ; app.get('/', (req, res) => {;   res.send('Hello World!');; }); ; app.listen(port, () => {;   console.log(`Example app listening on port ${port}`);; }); ; // Run with: node app.js  -> open http://localhost:3000/
idioms: Routing by HTTP method: app.get('/path', handler), plus app.post/app.put/app.delete, app.all() for any method, and app.METHOD chaining.

Route parameters: define named segments like '/users/:userId/books/:bookId' and read them from req.params (e.g. req.params.userId). In Express 5, wildcards must be named ('/files/*filepath') and optional segments use braces ('/path{/:opt}').
Application-level middleware with app.use((req, res, next) => { ... next(); }); always call next() (or end the response) so the request does not hang.

Built-in body/asset middleware: app.use(express.json()) for JSON bodies, app.use(express.urlencoded({ extended: true })) for form bodies, and app.use(express.static('public')) to serve static files. No more body-parser dependency.
Modular routers: const router = express.Router(); define router.get(...) etc., module.exports = router, then mount with app.use('/birds', router) to namespace routes.

Error-handling middleware uses exactly four args (err, req, res, next) and is registered LAST with app.use; in Express 5, rejected promises / errors thrown in async route handlers are forwarded to it automatically.
Sending responses: res.send(body), res.json(obj), res.status(code) (chainable, e.g. res.status(404).json({...})), res.redirect(url), and res.sendFile(path).
when_to_use: Use Express when you want a fast, minimal, unopinionated Node.js framework to build HTTP APIs and web servers with flexible routing and a middleware pipeline.

install: npm install zustand   (latest: v5.0.14 as of mid-2026; also: pnpm add zustand / yarn add zustand)
quickstart:
// Create a store (the store IS a hook), then consume slices in components — no Provider.; import { create } from 'zustand'; ; const useBearStore = create((set) => ({; bears: 0,; increasePopulation: () => set((state) => ({ bears: state.bears + 1 })),; removeAllBears: () => set({ bears: 0 }),; })); ; function BearCounter() {; const bears = useBearStore((state) => state.bears) // select narrow slice; return

{bears} around here ...

; }; ; function Controls() {; const increasePopulation = useBearStore((s) => s.increasePopulation); return ; }
idioms: Always select narrow slices: const bears = useStore((s) => s.bears). Selecting the whole store (no selector) re-renders on every change; selectors use strict-equality (Object.is) to skip renders.

Select multiple values without extra re-renders via useShallow: import { useShallow } from 'zustand/react/shallow'; const { nuts, honey } = useStore(useShallow((s) => ({ nuts: s.nuts, honey: s.honey }))).

Co-locate actions inside the store and update with set, which shallow-merges at the top level: set((state) => ({ bears: state.bears + 1 })) or set({ bears: 0 }). Use get() inside actions to read current state.
Async actions need no special handling — call set after the await: addBears: async () => { const r = await fetch(...); set({ bears: await r.json() }) }.
Read/write outside React (event handlers, vanilla code, tests) with useStore.getState() and useStore.setState(...); react to changes with const unsub = useStore.subscribe(listener).

Persist state across sessions with the persist middleware: import { persist, createJSONStorage } from 'zustand/middleware'; create()(persist((set,get)=>({...}), { name: 'app-storage', storage: createJSONStorage(() => localStorage) })). Use partialize to persist only chosen fields.

Write mutable-style updates for deep/nested state via the immer middleware: import { immer } from 'zustand/middleware/immer'; create(immer((set) => ({ updateNested: () => set((s) => { s.deep.nested.value++ }) }))).
TypeScript: annotate with the curried form create<State>()((set) => ({...})) — note the extra () — because TS cannot infer the state type from a single call.
when_to_use: When you need a lightweight, hook-based, boilerplate-free global state store for React (no Provider/Context wrapping) that scales from a small shared slice to app-wide state with optional persistence/immer middleware.

install: Desktop (Mac/Windows/Linux): download and run the installer from https://docs.docker.com/get-started/get-docker/. Linux Engine via apt (Ubuntu): set up Docker's apt repo, then `sudo apt install docker-ce docker-ce-cli containerd.io docker-buildx-plugin docker-compose-plugin`. Dev/test convenience script: `curl -fsSL https://get.docker.com -o get-docker.sh && sudo sh get-docker.sh`. Verify with `docker run hello-world`.
quickstart: # 1. Write a Dockerfile in your project root; FROM node:22-alpine; WORKDIR /app; COPY . .; RUN yarn install --production; CMD ["node", "./src/index.js"]; ; # 2. Build an image and tag it; docker build -t getting-started .; ; # 3. Run the container detached, mapping host:container ports; docker run -d -p 127.0.0.1:3000:3000 getting-started; ; # 4. Inspect / stop; docker ps; docker stop <container-id>; ; # Verify the engine is working at any time; docker run hello-world
idioms: Build and tag images from a Dockerfile: `docker build -t name:tag .`; push/pull from a registry with `docker push name:tag` / `docker pull name:tag` (defaults to Docker Hub).
Run containers with the common flags `-d` (detached), `-p host:container` (publish port), `--name` (friendly name), `-e KEY=value` (env var), and `--rm` (auto-remove on exit).
Manage lifecycle: `docker ps` / `docker ps -a` to list, `docker logs <ctr>`, `docker exec -it <ctr> sh` to get a shell inside a running container, `docker stop`/`docker rm`/`docker rmi` to clean up.
Persist and share data with named volumes (`-v myvol:/data`) for durable storage and bind mounts (`-v $(pwd):/app`) for live-mounting source code during development.
Orchestrate multi-container apps declaratively with a `compose.yaml` and `docker compose up` / `docker compose down`; use `docker compose watch` for live-reload dev workflows.

Optimize images with multi-stage builds (multiple `FROM` stages), a `.dockerignore` file, small base images (e.g. `-alpine`), and ordering layers so dependency installs are cached before copying source.
Reclaim disk and tidy up with `docker system prune` (dangling images/stopped containers) and inspect usage via `docker system df`.
when_to_use: Use Docker to package an application and its dependencies into portable, reproducible containers that run consistently across development, CI, and production.

install: built into react (>=16.8)
quickstart: const [state, setState] = useState(initial); useEffect(() => { /* effect */ return () => {/* cleanup */}; }, [deps]);
idioms: useState(initial); update from previous with setState(prev => next)
useEffect(fn, deps): side effects; return cleanup; [] runs once on mount
useRef(initial): mutable value / DOM ref without causing re-render
useMemo(() => compute, deps) and useCallback(fn, deps) to memoize values/functions
useContext(MyContext) to read context; useReducer(reducer, init) for complex state
Rules of Hooks: only call at the top level of a component/hook, never inside loops/conditions
Custom hook = a function named useXxx that composes built-in hooks and returns state+handlers (e.g. useFetch, useDebounce, useLocalStorage, useToggle)
when_to_use: State, side effects, and reusable stateful logic in React function components.

install: Bundled with Docker Desktop / docker-compose-plugin
quickstart: compose.yaml defines services (api, db); `docker compose up -d --build`; `docker compose logs -f`
idioms: depends_on with condition: service_healthy + a db healthcheck (pg_isready)
named volumes for postgres data persistence
env via environment:/env_file:, never hardcode secrets
one service per process; build: <dir> or image:
docker compose pull && up -d for zero-touch redeploy
when_to_use: Local dev and small single-host deploys of multi-container apps (api+db+redis).

install: uv add gunicorn
quickstart: gunicorn app.main:app -k uvicorn.workers.UvicornWorker -w 4 -b 0.0.0.0:8000
idioms: -k uvicorn.workers.UvicornWorker to run an ASGI app (FastAPI)
-w workers ~= 2*CPU cores + 1
--timeout / --graceful-timeout for slow requests + clean shutdown
front with a reverse proxy; pass --proxy-headers via the worker
prefer for multi-worker prod; plain uvicorn --workers N also works
when_to_use: Production process manager for ASGI/WSGI Python apps with multiple workers.

install: uv add 'uvicorn[standard]'
quickstart: uvicorn app.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 (dev: --reload)
idioms: --reload for dev only
--workers N for multi-process (or run under gunicorn)
--proxy-headers --forwarded-allow-ips='*' behind a proxy
lifespan events for startup/shutdown (DB pool)
ASGI server; pair with gunicorn worker class in prod
when_to_use: ASGI server for FastAPI/Starlette in dev and prod.

install: uv add alembic
quickstart: alembic init -t async migrations; alembic revision --autogenerate -m 'msg'; alembic upgrade head
idioms: autogenerate from SQLAlchemy metadata, then REVIEW the migration
run `alembic upgrade head` as a pre-release deploy step (not at app start)
async template for async engines
keep migrations in version control; one head
downgrade paths for rollback
when_to_use: Schema migrations for SQLAlchemy/PostgreSQL across deploys.

quickstart: Caddyfile: `example.com { reverse_proxy /api/* localhost:8000; root * /srv; file_server; try_files {path} /index.html }`; `caddy run`
idioms: automatic HTTPS via Let's Encrypt (zero config)
reverse_proxy to the API; file_server + try_files for the SPA
simplest TLS of the proxies
Caddyfile or JSON config
docker: mount Caddyfile + data volume for certs
when_to_use: Reverse proxy + automatic HTTPS for an API+SPA with minimal config.

install: apt install nginx / docker image nginx:alpine
quickstart: server { location /api/ { proxy_pass http://api:8000; } location / { root /usr/share/nginx/html; try_files $uri /index.html; } }
idioms: try_files $uri /index.html for SPA history routing
proxy_pass + proxy_set_header Host/X-Forwarded-* for the API
immutable cache headers for hashed assets
gzip/brotli for text assets
TLS via certbot/Let's Encrypt (manual vs Caddy's auto)
when_to_use: Battle-tested static server + reverse proxy (manual TLS).

install: Install flyctl; fly auth login
quickstart: fly launch (detects Dockerfile, writes fly.toml); fly deploy; fly postgres create + fly postgres attach
idioms: Docker-native deploys to global VMs
managed Postgres via `fly postgres`
secrets via `fly secrets set KEY=val`
fly.toml for [http_service] + healthchecks
scale with `fly scale count/vm`

install: Connect GitHub repo (or `railway` CLI)
quickstart: New project → deploy from repo; add a PostgreSQL plugin; set variables; auto-deploys on push
idioms: managed Postgres/Redis as plugins
env vars + references between services
auto-deploy on git push
Nixpacks or Dockerfile builds
good for API + DB PaaS
when_to_use: Fastest PaaS for an API + managed database from a repo.

quickstart: Create a Web Service (Docker or native) + a managed PostgreSQL; set env vars; auto-deploy on push
idioms: render.yaml (Blueprint) for infra-as-code
managed Postgres + free TLS + health checks
background workers / cron jobs
auto-deploy on push
static site type for the SPA

quickstart: Import the repo; framework preset = Vite; build `npm run build`, output `dist`; set VITE_* envs; deploy
idioms: git push → preview + prod deploys on CDN
VITE_* build-time env (public only)
SPA rewrites to /index.html
great for the React/Vite frontend
serverless/edge functions if needed

install: Add .github/workflows/*.yml
quickstart: on: push -> jobs: lint+test, build Docker image, push to registry, deploy (CLI/SSH)
idioms: astral-sh/setup-uv + uv sync --locked for Python CI
matrix across Python/Node versions
cache deps; build+push image with docker/build-push-action
deploy step gated on main / a protected Environment
secrets via repo/Environment secrets (never in YAML)
when_to_use: CI/CD: test on PR, build+deploy on merge.

install: uv add httpx  (or: pip install httpx; add httpx[http2] for HTTP/2)
quickstart: import httpx; r = httpx.get('https://example.com'); r.raise_for_status(); print(r.json())   # async: async with httpx.AsyncClient() as c: r = await c.get(url)
idioms: Reuse a Client/AsyncClient (connection pooling) instead of top-level httpx.get/post; use it as a context manager so connections close cleanly.
Pass timeout=httpx.Timeout(10.0, connect=5.0) explicitly — httpx applies a 5s default timeout (unlike requests which has none).
Call r.raise_for_status() to turn 4xx/5xx into httpx.HTTPStatusError; catch httpx.RequestError for transport failures.
Same API for sync and async: httpx.Client vs httpx.AsyncClient, with await on request methods in the async variant.
Set base_url, default headers, and auth on the Client once: httpx.Client(base_url='https://api.x', headers={...}, auth=('u','p')).
Stream large responses with `with client.stream('GET', url) as r: for chunk in r.iter_bytes(): ...` to avoid loading the whole body.
when_to_use: Choose httpx when you need a single HTTP client API that works in both sync and async code, HTTP/2, or strict timeouts by default; it is the modern successor to requests and the natural client for asyncio/FastAPI apps. Prefer plain requests for simple synchronous scripts where its huge ecosystem and ubiquity matter and async/HTTP-2 are not needed; prefer aiohttp when you also need an async HTTP server or want a battle-tested pure-async stack.

install: uv add requests  (or: pip install requests)
quickstart: import requests; r = requests.get('https://api.github.com', params={'q': 'x'}, timeout=10); r.raise_for_status(); data = r.json()
idioms: ALWAYS pass timeout=; requests has no default timeout and will hang forever otherwise.
Use a requests.Session() for multiple requests to one host to reuse TCP connections and persist cookies/headers.
Call r.raise_for_status() to raise requests.HTTPError on 4xx/5xx instead of silently continuing.
Send JSON with json=payload (sets Content-Type automatically); send form data with data=dict; files with files=.
Catch requests.exceptions.RequestException as the base class for connection, timeout, and HTTP errors.
Mount an HTTPAdapter with urllib3 Retry on a Session for transparent retry/backoff on idempotent calls.
when_to_use: Choose requests for straightforward synchronous HTTP in scripts, CLIs, and small services where simplicity and a vast ecosystem of helpers/examples win. Prefer httpx if you need async support, HTTP/2, or sane default timeouts; prefer aiohttp for high-concurrency async clients or when you need an async server too. requests is synchronous only and will block an event loop, so never call it from async code.

install: uv add aiohttp  (or: pip install aiohttp[speedups]  for aiodns + Brotli)
quickstart: import aiohttp, asyncio; async def main():     async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as s:         async with s.get('https://example.com') as r:             print(r.status, await r.json()); asyncio.run(main())
idioms: Create one ClientSession for the app/lifetime and reuse it — do NOT open a session per request; close it with `async with` or await session.close().
Responses are context managers: `async with session.get(url) as resp:` then await resp.text()/resp.json()/resp.read().
Set timeouts with aiohttp.ClientTimeout(total=30) passed to the session or per request.
Build async web servers with aiohttp.web: define handlers, add routes via app.router.add_get, run with web.run_app(app).
Use TCPConnector(limit=...) to cap concurrent connections and install aiodns (speedups extra) for faster DNS.
Stream uploads/downloads with resp.content.iter_chunked(n) and pass data=open-file or async generators rather than buffering.
when_to_use: Choose aiohttp for fully asynchronous, high-concurrency HTTP clients and when you want a mature async HTTP/WebSocket server in the same library. Prefer httpx if you want one API spanning sync and async, HTTP/2, or a cleaner requests-like interface; prefer requests for simple synchronous work. aiohttp is async-only and requires an event loop.

install: uv add 'celery[redis]'  (or: pip install "celery[redis]" / "celery[librabbitmq]"; pick the broker extra you use)
quickstart: # tasks.py; from celery import Celery; app = Celery('proj', broker='redis://localhost:6379/0', backend='redis://localhost:6379/1'); @app.task; def add(x, y): return x + y; # run worker: celery -A tasks worker -l info ; enqueue: add.delay(2, 3)
idioms: Define tasks with the @app.task decorator and enqueue with .delay(args) or .apply_async(args=, countdown=, eta=, queue=).
Configure a result backend only if you need return values/status; many fire-and-forget pipelines run backend-less.
Run workers with `celery -A proj worker -l info`; schedule periodic tasks with celery beat and app.conf.beat_schedule.
Make tasks idempotent and retry transient failures with bind=True + self.retry(exc=e, countdown=..., max_retries=...).
Compose workflows with canvas primitives: chain(), group(), and chord() for fan-out/fan-in.
Set acks_late=True and prefetch limits for long tasks so unfinished work is redelivered if a worker dies.
when_to_use: Choose Celery for a mature, full-featured distributed task queue: scheduled jobs, retries, routing, workflows, and multiple brokers (Redis/RabbitMQ) across many workers. Prefer a lighter library (e.g. RQ or Dramatiq) for simple Redis-backed background jobs where Celery's configuration surface is overkill; prefer a managed queue/stream (SQS, Cloud Tasks) if you want to avoid running and monitoring your own workers and broker.

install: uv add redis  (or: pip install redis; add redis[hiredis] for faster parsing)
quickstart: import redis; r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, db=0, decode_responses=True); r.set('key', 'value'); print(r.get('key'))   # async: from redis.asyncio import Redis; await r.get('key')
idioms: Use decode_responses=True to get str instead of bytes back from Redis.
Prefer redis.Redis.from_url('redis://...') or a shared ConnectionPool; the client is thread-safe and pools connections automatically.
Use the async client via `from redis.asyncio import Redis` — same command API with await.
Batch round-trips with a pipeline: `with r.pipeline() as pipe: pipe.set(...); pipe.incr(...); pipe.execute()`.
Do atomic read-modify-write with WATCH/MULTI transactions or Lua via r.eval/register_script.
Use pub/sub via r.pubsub() and subscribe(), and set key TTLs with ex=/px= on set or r.expire().
when_to_use: Choose redis-py (the official client) for any Python access to Redis: caching, rate limiting, sessions, queues, pub/sub, and as a Celery broker/backend. Use the redis.asyncio module in asyncio apps and the sync client elsewhere. For very large clusters consider RedisCluster support built into redis-py; reach for a higher-level cache wrapper only if you want decorators/serialization rather than raw commands.

install: uv add tenacity  (or: pip install tenacity)
quickstart: from tenacity import retry, stop_after_attempt, wait_exponential; @retry(stop=stop_after_attempt(5), wait=wait_exponential(multiplier=1, max=10)); def call(): return do_flaky_thing()
idioms: Bound retries with stop=stop_after_attempt(n) or stop_after_delay(seconds) — never retry forever in production.
Use wait=wait_exponential(...) (optionally + wait_random for jitter) to back off instead of hammering a failing service.
Scope which errors retry with retry=retry_if_exception_type(SomeError) so you don't mask programming bugs.
Decorate async functions the same way — @retry works on coroutines and awaits between attempts.
Re-raise the original error after exhaustion with reraise=True instead of getting a RetryError wrapper.
For ad-hoc control use the Retrying/AsyncRetrying iterator: `for attempt in Retrying(...): with attempt: ...`.
when_to_use: Choose tenacity to add configurable retry/backoff to flaky calls (network, DB, third-party APIs) in both sync and async code, when you want declarative stop/wait/retry policies. Prefer a client's built-in retry (e.g. urllib3 Retry on a requests Session, or httpx transport retries) for pure HTTP transport-level retries; use tenacity when you need retries around arbitrary functions or custom conditions. Avoid retrying non-idempotent operations without care.

install: uv add structlog  (or: pip install structlog; add rich for pretty dev tracebacks)
quickstart: import structlog; log = structlog.get_logger(); log.info('user_login', user_id=123, ip='10.0.0.1')
idioms: Log events as a name plus key-value pairs (log.info('event', key=val)) rather than interpolated message strings.
Bind persistent context with log = log.bind(request_id=rid) so every later call carries those fields.
Use contextvars (structlog.contextlib / bind_contextvars) to attach request-scoped context across async tasks.
Configure once at startup via structlog.configure(processors=[...]) with a JSONRenderer in prod and ConsoleRenderer in dev.
Add processors like add_log_level, TimeStamper(fmt='iso'), and format_exc_info to enrich every event.
Integrate with stdlib logging via structlog.stdlib.ProcessorFormatter so library logs share the same output format.
when_to_use: Choose structlog when you want structured, machine-parseable (JSON) logs with bound context for services, observability pipelines, and async apps. Prefer the stdlib logging module alone for tiny scripts or when you must not add a dependency; structlog can wrap/augment stdlib logging when you need both. Consider loguru instead if you want a simpler, opinionated single-import logger without explicit structured-logging configuration.

install: uv add asyncpg  (or: pip install asyncpg)
quickstart: import asyncio, asyncpg; async def main():     conn = await asyncpg.connect('postgresql://user@localhost/db')     row = await conn.fetchrow('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id=$1', 1)     await conn.close(); asyncio.run(main())
idioms: Use numbered placeholders $1, $2 (PostgreSQL style), never %s or Python string formatting — this prevents SQL injection.
For servers use a pool: `pool = await asyncpg.create_pool(dsn); async with pool.acquire() as conn: ...`.
Pick the right fetch method: fetch() -> list of Records, fetchrow() -> one Record, fetchval() -> single scalar, execute() for writes.
Run multiple statements atomically inside `async with conn.transaction():`.
Bulk-insert efficiently with conn.executemany() or conn.copy_records_to_table() instead of looping single inserts.
Records support both index and key access (row['col']); register custom codecs with set_type_codec for special types (e.g. JSON).
when_to_use: Choose asyncpg for high-performance asyncio access directly to PostgreSQL; it is significantly faster than psycopg via its native binary-protocol implementation and is ideal for async services that hand-write SQL. Prefer SQLAlchemy (async engine, which can use asyncpg as its driver) when you want an ORM/Core abstraction and portability across databases; prefer psycopg (3) if you need a sync API or broader DB-API compatibility. PostgreSQL-only and async-only.

install: uv add numpy  (or: pip install numpy)
quickstart: import numpy as np; a = np.array([[1,2],[3,4]]); print(a @ a.T, a.mean(axis=0))
idioms: Vectorize over explicit Python loops: a * b, np.where(cond, x, y), np.sum(a, axis=...) instead of element-wise iteration.
Use broadcasting to combine arrays of different shapes (e.g. a[:, None] + b[None, :]) rather than tiling/repeating.
Prefer views and in-place ops (a[::2], a += 1, out=) to avoid copies; use a.copy() when you need to detach.
Boolean and fancy indexing for selection: a[a > 0], a[idx_array]; np.nonzero / np.argwhere to get indices.
Set dtype explicitly for memory/perf (np.float32, np.int64); watch silent upcasting and integer overflow.
Use a stable RNG via rng = np.random.default_rng(seed) instead of the legacy np.random.* global functions.
when_to_use: Foundational n-dimensional array and numerical computing layer for almost all Python scientific/ML code; choose it for dense numeric arrays, linear algebra, and as the interchange format other libraries build on. Prefer pandas/polars for labeled/tabular heterogeneous data, scipy.sparse for sparse matrices, and PyTorch/JAX/CuPy when you need autograd or GPU acceleration.

install: uv add pandas  (or: pip install pandas)
quickstart: import pandas as pd; df = pd.read_csv('data.csv'); print(df.groupby('k')['v'].mean())
idioms: Select rows/columns by label with .loc[] and by integer position with .iloc[]; avoid chained indexing like df[mask]['col'] = ... (use .loc).
Use vectorized column ops and .assign() for new columns instead of row-wise .apply / iterrows, which are slow.
Aggregate with groupby(...).agg({...}) and reshape with pivot_table / melt / stack / unstack.
Use method chaining (df.query(...).assign(...).groupby(...)) for readable pipelines; .pipe() to insert custom functions.
Handle missing data explicitly: isna(), dropna(), fillna(); prefer nullable / pyarrow-backed dtypes for clean NA semantics.
Parse dates with parse_dates= or pd.to_datetime, then use .dt accessors and resample() for time series.
when_to_use: The default for labeled, heterogeneous tabular data analysis, ETL, and time series in Python, with the largest ecosystem and I/O support (CSV/Parquet/SQL/Excel). Prefer polars for large datasets or speed-critical/lazy pipelines, and DuckDB/Dask/Spark when data exceeds single-machine memory.

install: uv add polars  (or: pip install polars; use polars-lts-cpu for older CPUs lacking AVX2)
quickstart: import polars as pl; df = pl.read_csv('data.csv'); print(df.group_by('k').agg(pl.col('v').mean()))
idioms: Build queries with the expression API inside select/filter/with_columns/group_by: pl.col('x').sum().over('g').
Use the lazy API (pl.scan_csv/scan_parquet -> .collect()) so the query optimizer can push down predicates and projections.
Chain transformations as expressions instead of Python loops or .map_elements (UDFs), which break parallelism.
Express conditionals with pl.when(cond).then(a).otherwise(b) and combine columns with arithmetic on pl.col.
Use .over() for window/group-wise computations and joins (df.join(other, on=, how=)) for combining frames.
Convert at boundaries with .to_pandas() / pl.from_pandas() or to Arrow for zero-copy interchange.
when_to_use: Choose polars for fast, memory-efficient DataFrame work on medium-to-large data, lazy/optimized query pipelines, and multithreaded performance without leaving Python. Prefer pandas when you need its mature ecosystem, niche I/O, or integrations (statsmodels, many plotting/ML helpers) that expect pandas objects; use DuckDB/Spark for out-of-core or distributed scale.

install: uv add scikit-learn  (import as sklearn; or: pip install -U scikit-learn)
quickstart: from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier; from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split; Xtr,Xte,ytr,yte = train_test_split(X,y); clf = RandomForestClassifier().fit(Xtr,ytr); clf.score(Xte,yte)
idioms: Follow the estimator API uniformly: .fit(X, y), .predict(X), .transform(X), .score(X, y).
Wrap preprocessing + model in a Pipeline (and ColumnTransformer for mixed columns) so transforms fit only on training folds and prevent leakage.
Tune hyperparameters with GridSearchCV / RandomizedSearchCV plus cross_val_score / cross_validate.
Scale features (StandardScaler) for distance/gradient-based models; trees and ensembles don't need it.
Use train_test_split with stratify= for classification and set random_state for reproducibility.
Evaluate with metrics fit to the task (classification_report, roc_auc_score, mean_squared_error) rather than accuracy alone.
when_to_use: Go-to for classical/tabular machine learning (linear models, trees, ensembles, SVMs, clustering, dimensionality reduction) and end-to-end preprocessing pipelines on in-memory data. Prefer XGBoost/LightGBM for top gradient-boosting performance and PyTorch/TensorFlow for deep learning, large-scale, or GPU-trained models.

install: pip install torch torchvision torchaudio  (use the selector at https://pytorch.org/get-started/locally/ for the correct CUDA/CPU build; import as torch)
quickstart: import torch, torch.nn as nn; model = nn.Linear(4, 2); x = torch.randn(8, 4); loss = model(x).sum(); loss.backward()
idioms: Subclass nn.Module and implement forward(); compose layers with nn.Sequential / nn.ModuleList.
Standard training loop: optimizer.zero_grad(); loss.backward(); optimizer.step(), with a DataLoader feeding batches.
Move tensors and model to a device explicitly: device = 'cuda' if torch.cuda.is_available() else 'cpu'; x = x.to(device).
Use torch.no_grad() (or inference_mode()) and model.eval() for evaluation/inference to disable grad and switch BatchNorm/Dropout.
Save/load via state dicts: torch.save(model.state_dict(), path) then model.load_state_dict(torch.load(path)).
Speed up with mixed precision (torch.autocast + GradScaler) and torch.compile() on supported models.
when_to_use: Primary framework for deep learning research and production: building/training neural networks, GPU acceleration, and autograd with a flexible eager API. Prefer scikit-learn for classical/tabular ML, ONNX Runtime for lightweight cross-platform inference of exported models, and JAX when you want functional transforms (vmap/grad/jit) or TPU-first workflows.

install: pip install opencv-python  (import as cv2; use opencv-python-headless on servers/containers, opencv-contrib-python for extra modules — install only ONE)
quickstart: import cv2; img = cv2.imread('in.jpg'); gray = cv2.cvtColor(img, cv2.COLOR_BGR2GRAY); cv2.imwrite('out.png', gray)
idioms: Remember OpenCV loads images as BGR (not RGB) NumPy arrays; convert with cv2.cvtColor before passing to RGB-based libs/models.
Images are H x W x C ndarrays, so use NumPy slicing for crops/ROIs and numeric ops directly on the array.
Always check that cv2.imread returned a non-None array (silent failure on missing/unsupported files).
Use cv2.resize, cv2.warpAffine/getRotationMatrix2D, and cv2.GaussianBlur/threshold for geometric and filtering ops.
Read video frames in a loop with cap = cv2.VideoCapture(src); ret, frame = cap.read(); release with cap.release().
Use the headless package in non-GUI environments to avoid cv2.imshow / Qt dependency errors.
when_to_use: Choose for fast, classical computer vision and image/video processing: filtering, transforms, feature detection, contours, camera/video I/O, and real-time pipelines. Prefer Pillow for simple image file manipulation and format conversion, and a deep-learning framework (PyTorch + torchvision) for learned vision models, using OpenCV mainly for pre/post-processing.

install: uv add pillow  (import as PIL, e.g. from PIL import Image; or: pip install Pillow)
quickstart: from PIL import Image; im = Image.open('in.jpg'); im.thumbnail((256,256)); im.convert('RGB').save('out.png')
idioms: Open lazily with Image.open() (data loads on first use); call im.load() if you close the file handle early.
Use convert() to change modes ('RGB','RGBA','L','1') and the format follows the save() extension or format= argument.
Resize/crop/rotate via im.resize((w,h)), im.crop(box), im.rotate(deg); use im.thumbnail() for in-place aspect-preserving downscale.
Bridge to NumPy with np.asarray(im) and back with Image.fromarray(arr) for pixel-level processing.
Compose and annotate using ImageDraw, ImageFont, and ImageFilter; paste with im.paste(layer, box, mask).
Use a context manager (with Image.open(p) as im:) to ensure file handles are released.
when_to_use: Pick Pillow for straightforward image loading/saving, format conversion, thumbnails, cropping, basic drawing, and metadata across many file formats. Prefer OpenCV for heavy/real-time computer vision, video, and advanced filtering, and scikit-image when you need scientific image-analysis algorithms.

install: pip install onnxruntime  (CPU; use onnxruntime-gpu for CUDA — install only ONE; import as onnxruntime)
quickstart: import onnxruntime as ort, numpy as np; sess = ort.InferenceSession('model.onnx', providers=['CPUExecutionProvider']); out = sess.run(None, {sess.get_inputs()[0].name: np.zeros((1,3,224,224), np.float32)})
idioms: Create one InferenceSession per model and reuse it across calls; session creation is expensive, run() is cheap.
Pass inputs as a dict keyed by the model's input names (sess.get_inputs()[i].name) with matching dtype/shape (often float32).
Select hardware via the providers list, e.g. ['CUDAExecutionProvider','CPUExecutionProvider'], falling back in order.
Use run(None, feeds) to fetch all outputs, or pass a list of output names to fetch a subset.
Inspect the model contract with get_inputs()/get_outputs() (name, shape, type) before feeding data.
Tune throughput with SessionOptions (intra/inter-op threads, graph optimization level) and use the headless/CPU package for small deployments.
when_to_use: Use ONNX Runtime for fast, portable inference of trained models exported to ONNX, across CPU/GPU and many platforms, decoupled from the training framework. Prefer keeping models in PyTorch/TensorFlow when you still need training or framework-specific ops; reach for TensorRT or vendor runtimes when you need maximum GPU-specific optimization.

install: uv add matplotlib  (import as import matplotlib.pyplot as plt; or: pip install -U matplotlib)
quickstart: import matplotlib.pyplot as plt; fig, ax = plt.subplots(); ax.plot([1,2,3],[1,4,9]); fig.savefig('plot.png')
idioms: Prefer the explicit object-oriented API (fig, ax = plt.subplots(); ax.plot(...)) over the stateful pyplot plt.plot interface.
Lay out multiple panels with plt.subplots(nrows, ncols) and use fig.tight_layout() or constrained_layout to avoid overlap.
Set labels/limits/scale on the Axes: ax.set_xlabel(...), ax.set_title(...), ax.set_ylim(...), ax.set_yscale('log').
Add ax.legend() (with label= on plot calls) and fig.colorbar(mappable, ax=ax) for legends and colorbars.
Save with fig.savefig(path, dpi=, bbox_inches='tight') and call plt.close(fig) in loops/servers to free memory.
Pick a non-interactive backend (matplotlib.use('Agg')) for headless scripts/servers before importing pyplot.
when_to_use: The foundational, highly customizable Python plotting library for static figures and publication-quality output, with broad control over every element. Prefer seaborn for concise statistical charts (it sits on top of matplotlib), and Plotly/Bokeh/Altair for interactive or web-based dashboards.

install: uv add typer  (or: pip install typer)
quickstart: import typer; app = typer.Typer(); @app.command(); def hello(name: str, count: int = 1, formal: bool = False):     for _ in range(count):         typer.echo(f"Good day {name}" if formal else f"Hi {name}"); if __name__ == "__main__":     app()
idioms: Declare CLI args/options purely via type hints: a parameter with no default becomes a positional Argument, one with a default becomes an Option; use typing.Annotated[T, typer.Option(...)] / typer.Argument(...) to attach help text, prompts, envvar, validation.
Build multi-command apps with @app.command(); nest sub-apps via app.add_typer(other_app, name="sub"); a single-command Typer collapses to a flat CLI automatically.
Use Enum subclasses for choice options, pathlib.Path for file args (with exists=True/dir_okay=False checks), and bool flags auto-generate --flag/--no-flag.
Control exit and output with typer.Exit(code=1), typer.Abort(), typer.echo()/typer.secho(..., fg=typer.colors.RED), and typer.confirm()/typer.prompt() for interaction.

Typer is built on Click, so callbacks (@app.callback for group-level options), context (typer.Context), and progress bars (typer.progressbar) work; rich is an optional dep that auto-formats errors and help.
Test commands with typer.testing.CliRunner().invoke(app, ["hello", "Bob"]) and assert on result.exit_code / result.stdout.
when_to_use: Choose Typer for new Python CLIs when you want the least boilerplate and type-hint-driven parsing with auto-generated help, shell completion, and pretty errors. Prefer plain Click when you need fine-grained control over Click's decorator/parameter machinery or are extending an existing Click codebase; prefer argparse (stdlib) when you must avoid third-party dependencies entirely.

install: uv add click  (or: pip install click)
quickstart: import click; @click.command(); @click.option("--count", default=1, help="Times to greet."); @click.argument("name"); def hello(count, name):     """Greet NAME COUNT times."""     for _ in range(count):         click.echo(f"Hello {name}!"); if __name__ == "__main__":     hello()
idioms: Decorate functions with @click.command() plus @click.argument() (positional) and @click.option() (flags); options support type=, default=, multiple=True, nargs, prompt=, envvar=, and is_flag=True.
Compose subcommands with @click.group() and @cli.command(), or attach with group.add_command(cmd); share state down the tree via @click.pass_context / ctx.obj and the @click.pass_obj decorator.
Use built-in param types: click.Choice([...]), click.Path(exists=True), click.File("r"), click.IntRange(0, 10), and click.DateTime; raise click.BadParameter / click.UsageError for validation.
I/O helpers: click.echo() (handles Unicode/redirects), click.secho(fg="red"), click.style(), click.confirm(), click.prompt(), click.progressbar(), and click.get_current_context().
Control program flow with ctx.exit(code), raise click.Abort(), and ctx.fail(); decorate with @click.version_option() and @click.help_option().
Test via click.testing.CliRunner().invoke(cli, ["--count", "3", "Bob"]) and inspect result.exit_code / result.output.
when_to_use: Choose Click when you want a mature, explicit, composable decorator-based CLI toolkit with full control over parsing, groups, and custom param types. Prefer Typer if you'd rather drive everything from type hints with less boilerplate (Typer wraps Click); prefer stdlib argparse when you cannot add dependencies.

install: uv add rich  (or: pip install rich)
quickstart: from rich.console import Console; console = Console(); console.print("Hello", "[bold magenta]World[/bold magenta]!", ":vampire:"); console.log("Logged with timestamp", {"key": "value"})
idioms: Use a single Console() instance and console.print() with BBCode-style markup ([bold red]...[/]) for color/style; console.log() adds timestamps and source info for debugging.
Render structured output with rich.table.Table (add_column/add_row), rich.panel.Panel, rich.tree.Tree, rich.markdown.Markdown, and rich.syntax.Syntax for code highlighting.
Show progress with rich.progress.track(iterable, description=...) for simple loops, or the Progress context manager for multiple concurrent tasks; rich.status.Status / console.status() for spinners.
Install rich.traceback.install(show_locals=True) for prettier exceptions, and use rich.logging.RichHandler with the logging module for formatted log output.
Use rich.live.Live for continuously updating displays (dashboards), and rich.pretty / rich.print for syntax-highlighted pprint of data structures; rich.prompt.Prompt.ask() for typed input.
Capture or redirect output with Console(file=..., record=True) and console.export_text()/export_html()/export_svg(); width and color depth auto-detect but can be forced.
when_to_use: Choose Rich to add color, tables, progress bars, markdown, and beautiful tracebacks to terminal output or logs with minimal effort. Prefer it over manual ANSI codes or colorama for anything beyond basic coloring; if you need a full interactive TUI application (widgets, layouts, mouse/keyboard event loop), step up to Textual (same authors, built on Rich) instead.

install: uv add --dev pytest-asyncio  (or: pip install pytest-asyncio)
quickstart: import pytest; @pytest.mark.asyncio; async def test_fetch():     result = await some_async_func()     assert result == 42
idioms: Mark individual coroutine tests with @pytest.mark.asyncio so the plugin runs them on an event loop.
Set asyncio_mode = "auto" under [tool.pytest.ini_options] to auto-collect all async def tests without per-test markers; "strict" (default) requires explicit markers.
Write async fixtures as `@pytest_asyncio.fixture` with async def + yield; they are awaited and torn down on the same loop.
Pin the loop scope explicitly with @pytest.mark.asyncio(loop_scope="session"|"module"|...) (and configure asyncio_default_fixture_loop_scope) so fixtures and tests share the right loop.
Override the event loop or its policy via an event_loop_policy fixture rather than redefining event_loop (the old event_loop fixture is deprecated).
Requires Python 3.10+ and a recent pytest; declare it as a test dependency, not a runtime one.
when_to_use: Choose pytest-asyncio when your code uses the stdlib asyncio event loop and you want to write `async def` tests and fixtures with pytest. Prefer anyio's pytest plugin instead if your library targets multiple async backends (asyncio + Trio) or already uses AnyIO; use pytest-trio for Trio-only projects.

install: uv add --dev pytest-cov  (or: pip install pytest-cov)
quickstart: pytest --cov=my_package --cov-report=term-missing
idioms: Measure coverage by passing --cov=PACKAGE (repeatable for several packages/paths); omit the value to cover the whole project.
Pick report formats with --cov-report: term, term-missing (shows uncovered line numbers), html, xml, json, lcov, or annotate; combine multiple --cov-report flags.
Fail the build below a threshold with --cov-fail-under=90; reset accumulated data between runs with --cov-reset / append with --cov-append.
Configure source, omit, and exclude_lines via a coverage.py config ([tool.coverage.run] / [tool.coverage.report] in pyproject.toml); pytest-cov is a thin pytest wrapper over coverage.py.
Works with pytest-xdist parallel runs (coverage is collected per-worker and combined) and supports subprocess coverage via COVERAGE_PROCESS_START.
Use coverage contexts (--cov-context=test) to record which test exercised each line.
when_to_use: Choose pytest-cov to integrate coverage.py measurement and CI gating directly into a pytest run (single command, xdist-aware). Prefer invoking coverage.py directly (`coverage run -m pytest`) when you need full control over coverage's lifecycle or are measuring code paths outside pytest; pytest-cov is the convenient default for pytest projects.

install: uv add --dev hypothesis  (or: pip install hypothesis)
quickstart: from hypothesis import given, strategies as st; @given(st.lists(st.integers())); def test_sort_idempotent(xs):     assert sorted(sorted(xs)) == sorted(xs)
idioms: Drive tests with @given(strategy_or_kwargs); Hypothesis generates many inputs and, on failure, shrinks to a minimal reproducing example.
Compose data with the strategies module (st): integers/floats/text/booleans, lists/dictionaries/tuples/sets, sampled_from, one_of, none, and st.builds()/st.composite for custom objects.
Refine and transform strategies with .map(), .filter(), and st.recursive(); use st.data() inside a test to draw values interactively (data.draw(...)).
Tune runs and reproducibility with @settings(max_examples=..., deadline=..., derandomize=...); pin known regressions with @example(...).
Use assume(cond) to discard uninteresting inputs, and target() to guide the search toward edge cases; @given integrates cleanly inside pytest tests and fixtures.
Model sequences of operations with stateful testing (RuleBasedStateMachine + @rule); failing examples are cached in the .hypothesis database for replay.
when_to_use: Choose Hypothesis for property-based testing when you want to assert invariants over a wide, automatically generated input space (parsers, serializers, math, data structures) and get minimal counterexamples. Use it alongside, not instead of, example-based pytest tests: prefer plain parametrized tests for fixed, well-known cases and reach for Hypothesis when the input domain is large or edge cases are hard to enumerate by hand.

install: uv add sqlite-vec  (or: pip install sqlite-vec)
quickstart: import sqlite3, sqlite_vec; from sqlite_vec import serialize_float32; db = sqlite3.connect(":memory:"); db.enable_load_extension(True); sqlite_vec.load(db); db.enable_load_extension(False); db.execute("CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE items USING vec0(embedding float[4])"); db.execute("INSERT INTO items(rowid, embedding) VALUES (?, ?)", [1, serialize_float32([0.1,0.2,0.3,0.4])]); rows = db.execute("SELECT rowid, distance FROM items WHERE embedding MATCH ? ORDER BY distance LIMIT 3", [serialize_float32([0.1,0.2,0.3,0.4])]).fetchall()
idioms: Load the extension into a stdlib sqlite3 connection with sqlite_vec.load(db) after db.enable_load_extension(True) (the pip package ships the prebuilt binary); no server needed.
Create a vec0 virtual table declaring vector columns as `embedding float[N]` (N = dimensionality); also supports bit[N] (binary) and int8[N] (quantized) vectors for big space savings.
Serialize Python list vectors to the compact binary format with sqlite_vec.serialize_float32(list); pass NumPy arrays as .astype(np.float32) bytes.
Run KNN with `WHERE embedding MATCH ? ORDER BY distance LIMIT k`; it is exact brute-force search and you read the auto-provided `distance` column.
Add auxiliary/metadata columns in vec0 (e.g. `+title text`, partition keys) to filter alongside vector search; or store metadata in a normal table and JOIN on rowid.
Choose the distance metric per column via `distance_metric=cosine|L2` in the table definition; defaults to L2.
when_to_use: Choose sqlite-vec for fast, embedded, dependency-free vector search that lives inside a SQLite file — ideal for local apps, prototypes, edge/mobile, and RAG where you want vectors next to relational data with zero infrastructure. Prefer a dedicated vector DB (Qdrant, pgvector, Milvus, Weaviate) when you need approximate ANN indexes at very large scale, distributed/horizontal scaling, or a long-lived multi-client server.

install: uv add fastembed  (or: pip install fastembed)
quickstart: from fastembed import TextEmbedding; model = TextEmbedding()  # default: BAAI/bge-small-en-v1.5 (384-dim); embeddings = list(model.embed(["hello world", "another doc"]))  # generator of np.ndarray
idioms: TextEmbedding().embed(docs) returns a generator of float32 numpy arrays; wrap in list() or iterate. Use passage_embed()/query_embed() helpers for retrieval-optimized prefixes when the model expects them.

List available models with TextEmbedding.list_supported_models(); select a specific one via TextEmbedding(model_name="BAAI/bge-base-en-v1.5"); models are downloaded and cached locally and run on ONNX Runtime (no PyTorch/GPU required).
Generate embeddings in batches with embed(docs, batch_size=...) and parallelize with parallel=N (or 0 for all cores) for large corpora.
Beyond dense text use: SparseTextEmbedding (SPLADE / BM25-style sparse vectors), LateInteractionTextEmbedding (ColBERT), ImageEmbedding (CLIP-style), and TextCrossEncoder for reranking.
Pairs naturally with Qdrant: install qdrant-client[fastembed] to let the client embed and upsert/search in one step.
